content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
1 
(916} 322-5660 • Fax (916} 322-0886 
Heather L. Stroud 
Deputy General Counsel 
Office of the City Attorney 
1200 Carlsbad Village Dr. 
Carlsbad, CA 92008 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-237 
Dear Ms. Stroud: 
January 31, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et 
seq.1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or 
common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (111 re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. 
When providing advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 (c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 (c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Is a contractor initially hired by the Carlsbad Municipal Water District to provide design, 
project management, bidding, and construction services for the La Costa Water Main Relocation 
Project prohibited under Section 1090 from entering into a subsequent contract with the District to 
complete those services after the project had been put on hold? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the District from entering into a new contract to 
complete essentially the same services specified in a previous contract with the same contractor. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 
through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of 
the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-237 
Page No. 2 
In November 2012, the Carlsbad Municipal Water District (CMWD), a subsidiary district of 
the City of Carlsbad, entered into a master agreement with GHD, Inc. for water and recycled 
water engineering consulting services. Under that master agreement, CMWD issued a task order on 
July 2, 2013, for a term of 120 working days with total compensation not to exceed $80,780, to 
provide engineering design services for the La Costa Water Main Relocation Project to replace a 
public pipeline that had broken on private property. 
The scope of services under the task order consisted of: project administration and 
management; preliminary design services; final design services including preparation of 
construction plans, a construction cost estimate, and technical specifications; bidding support 
services; and construction support services including attending meetings with the selected 
contractor, responding to requests for information from the contractor, and reviewing proposed 
material submittals by the contractor. 
Pursuant to that task order, GHD completed the preliminary design portion of the scope of 
work, culminating in a final draft design. However, CMWD put the project on hold in mid-2014 
because of difficulties gaining permission to use private property outside of the CMWD easement 
needed for temporary construction access and because of staff turnover within CMWD. Now that 
CMWD is ready to finish the scope of work that was started under the original task order, the task 
order and the underlying master agreement have expired. The task order expired with 
approximately $30,000 remaining on the scope of work, as the final design and construction support 
work had not yet been performed. 
CMWD desires to continue to use GHD to finish the scope of work that remains. GHD has 
extensive project history and knowledge that makes it desirable for CMWD to continue to work 
with them, especially because the original CMWD project manager has left CMWD. Additionally, a 
new consultant would have to assume professional liability for GHD's design, which would create 
additional cost. 
Because the prior contract expired, CMWD proposes to enter into a new contract with GHD 
to complete the original scope of work. The only services under the new contract not specified in 
the original contract would be two additional meetings and a site visit totaling $4,025. The total 
compensation under the new contract would not exceed $34,890. This amount is under CMWD's 
$35,000 threshold requiring solicitation of three proposals for professional service agreements, so 
the contract could be awarded to GHD without a competitive process. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section I 090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 


File No. A-16-237 
Page No. 3 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section I 090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 
Based upon the facts provided, the pertinent question is whether the Section I 090 prohibits 
CMWD from entering into a contract with a contractor that had provided essentially the same 
services under a previously expired contract. 
For purposes of consultants and contractors, it has been determined that Section l 090 
applies to consultants who have "a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications" 
for a public project. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.) 
Accordingly, Section 1090 is generally applicable to contractors that serve in advisory positions and 
have the potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency. 
(See Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147 citing Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of 
Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; also see Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.) 
This application recognizes that contractors, who serve the public temporarily, still "owe the same 
fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal. Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
Further guiding our analysis of whether Section 1090 applies, we have previously found that 
the focus is on the scope of influence the contractor holds in advising the public entity. (See 
Chadwick, supra, p.12.) Moreover, "an individual's status as an official ... turns on the extent to 
which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that 
demands the public trust." (Hub City Solid Waste Sen1ices, Inc. v. City of Compton, supra, 186 
Cal.App.4th 1125.) d.) 
In this case, GHD was previously hired to design and develop the La Costa Water Main 
Relocation Project and to assist in selecting and reviewing a separate contractor to complete the 
project. While GHD's considerable influence on the design and development of the project would 
preclude GHD from subsequently bidding to complete the project, we do not find that Section I 090 
prohibits CMWD from hiring GHD to complete essentially the same work specified in its previous 
contract. In this regard, GHD has not exerted considerable influence over the agency's decision to 
complete the work specified in the expired contracts or to hire GHD to complete the work. 


File No. A-16-237 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

complete those services after the project had been put on hold?

No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the District from entering into a new contract to 
complete essentially the same services specified in a previous contract with the same contractor. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[ m ]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section I 090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering  
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section I 090 is void. (Thomson v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-

For purposes of consultants and contractors, it has been determined that Section l 090

fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal. Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
Further guiding our analysis of whether Section 1090 applies, we have previously found that

complete the work specified in the expired contracts or to hire GHD to complete the work.

project management, bidding, and construction services for the La Costa Water Main Relocation 
Project prohibited under Section 1090 from entering into a subsequent contract with the District to 
complete those services after the project had been put on hold?

CONCLUSION: No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the District from entering into a new contract to 
complete essentially the same services specified in a previous contract with the same contractor. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.
